text:
[PAGE 1]
Artificial  Intelligence  Governance:  
UNC
 
System
 
Framework
 
and
 
Elizabeth
 
City
 
State
 
University
 
(ECSU)
 
Policies
 
 
 
Executive  Summary  
Artificial  intelligence  (AI),  particularly  generative  AI  tools,  is  reshaping  teaching,  learning,  and  research  
in
 
higher
 
education.
 
Within
 
the
 
University
 
of
 
North
 
Carolina
 
(UNC)
 
System,
 
governance
 
of
 
AI
 
is
 
developing
 
through
 
a
 
distributed
 
framework
 
rather
 
than
 
a
 
single
 
comprehensive
 
regulation.
 
At  Elizabeth  City  State  University  (ECSU),  AI  use  is  managed  primarily  through:  
●  Academic  integrity  policies  ●  Student  conduct  rules  ●  Faculty-level  syllabus  policies  ●  Library  and  instructional  guidance  ●  Information  technology  and  data-security  practices  
The  UNC  System  provides  strategic  direction  and  shared  instructional  guidance,  while  ECSU  and  other  
campuses
 
implement
 
policies
 
locally.
 
This
 
layered
 
approach
 
reflects
 
broader
 
trends
 
in
 
higher
 
education
 
governance
 
of
 
emerging
 
technologies.
 
 
1.  UNC  System-Wide  Framework  for  Artificial  
Intelligence
 
1.1  Responsible  Integration  Rather  Than  Prohibition  
Across  UNC  institutions,  the  prevailing  philosophy  is  to  integrate  AI  responsibly  rather  than  prohibit  it.  
UNC-Chapel
 
Hill
 
faculty
 
guidance
 
encourages
 
instructors
 
to
 
define
 
appropriate
 
uses
 
of
 
AI
 
in
 
their
 
courses
 
and
 
embed
 
expectations
 
in
 
their
 
syllabi
 
(University
 
of
 
North
 
Carolina
 
at
 
Chapel
 
Hill,
 
Developing
 
AI
 
Guidance
 
for
 
Faculty
).
 

[PAGE 2]
This  approach  reflects  an  understanding  that  AI  tools  are  already  in  use  and  must  be  addressed  through  
structured
 
policy
 
rather
 
than
 
bans.
 
 
1.2  Faculty  Learning  Communities  and  System  Initiatives  
The  UNC  System  Office  sponsors  a  Generative  AI  in  Teaching  and  Learning  Faculty  Learning  
Community
 
(FLC)
 
to
 
support
 
cross-campus
 
collaboration
 
and
 
responsible
 
integration
 
of
 
AI
 
(University
 
of
 
North
 
Carolina
 
System
 
Office,
 
Faculty
 
Learning
 
Communities
).
 
The  FLC:  
●  Brings  together  faculty  and  instructional  professionals  ●  Explores  responsible  uses  of  generative  AI  ●  Develops  teaching  resources  ●  Shares  best  practices  across  institutions  
This  demonstrates  a  governance  model  focused  on  professional  development  and  shared  learning  rather  
than
 
centralized
 
mandates.
 
 
1.3  Instructional  Governance  and  Course-Level  Policies  
UNC-Chapel  Hill  provides  structured  guidance  encouraging  faculty  to:  
●  Reflect  on  learning  goals  ●  Define  acceptable  and  prohibited  AI  uses  ●  Require  disclosure  of  AI  use  ●  Clearly  communicate  expectations  in  syllabi  
(University  of  North  Carolina  at  Chapel  Hill,  Developing  AI  Guidance  for  Faculty )  
This  model  reinforces  faculty  authority  at  the  course  level.  
 
1.4  Core  Principles  in  UNC  Teaching  Guidance  
Human  Accountability  

[PAGE 3]
UNC  teaching  guidance  states  that  instructors  are  “100%  responsible”  for  their  teaching  materials  and  
must
 
verify
 
AI-generated
 
content
 
before
 
use
 
(University
 
of
 
North
 
Carolina
 
at
 
Chapel
 
Hill,
 
Teaching
 
Generative
 
AI
 
Usage
 
Guidance
).
 
Similarly,  students  are  responsible  for  evaluating  AI  outputs  and  ensuring  the  accuracy  of  their  final  
submissions
 
(UNC-Chapel
 
Hill,
 
Developing
 
AI
 
Guidance
 
for
 
Faculty
).
 
 
Transparency  and  Documentation  
UNC  guidance  encourages  transparency  about  AI  use  in  both  instructional  materials  and  student  
assignments
 
(UNC-Chapel
 
Hill,
 
Teaching
 
Generative
 
AI
 
Usage
 
Guidance
).
 
 
Privacy  and  Data  Protection  
UNC  guidance  explicitly  cautions  users  to  avoid  entering  confidential  or  personal  data  into  AI  tools  
(UNC-Chapel
 
Hill,
 
Teaching
 
Generative
 
AI
 
Usage
 
Guidance
).
 
 
Pedagogical  Adaptation  
UNC  recommends  redesigning  assessments  to  address  AI  capabilities,  including  process-based  learning  
and
 
in-class
 
assessments
 
(UNC-Chapel
 
Hill,
 
Teaching
 
Generative
 
AI
 
Usage
 
Guidance
).
 
 
2.  Elizabeth  City  State  University  (ECSU):  
Institutional
 
Policies
 
and
 
Framework
 
2.1  Academic  Integrity  as  the  Primary  Regulatory  Framework  
At  ECSU,  AI  use  is  governed  primarily  through  existing  academic  integrity  policies.  The  Student  Code  of  
Conduct
 
defines
 
plagiarism
 
as
 
using
 
another’s
 
work
 
without
 
acknowledgment
 
(Elizabeth
 
City
 
State
 
University,
 
Student
 
Code
 
of
 
Conduct
).
 
This  definition  applies  to  AI-generated  material  submitted  without  attribution.  

[PAGE 4]
ECSU  Library  guidance  reinforces  proper  citation  practices  and  explains  plagiarism  standards  (Elizabeth  
City
 
State
 
University
 
Library,
 
Academic
 
Integrity
 
and
 
Citation
 
Guidance
).
 
 
2.2  Faculty  Governance  and  Syllabus  Policies  
Like  UNC-Chapel  Hill,  ECSU  relies  on  faculty  to  define  course-level  AI  policies  through  syllabus  
statements
 
and
 
assignment
 
design
 
(ECSU
 
Student
 
Code
 
of
 
Conduct;
 
UNC-Chapel
 
Hill,
 
Developing
 
AI
 
Guidance
 
for
 
Faculty
).
 
 
2.3  Data  Privacy  and  Operational  Considerations  
While  ECSU  does  not  publish  a  standalone  AI  privacy  policy,  institutional  conduct  and  IT  policies  require  
responsible
 
use
 
of
 
university
 
systems
 
and
 
protection
 
of
 
student
 
information
 
(ECSU
 
Student
 
Code
 
of
 
Conduct).
 
This  aligns  with  UNC  guidance  cautioning  against  entering  sensitive  data  into  AI  systems  (UNC-Chapel  
Hill,
 
Teaching
 
Generative
 
AI
 
Usage
 
Guidance
).
 
 
2.4  Workforce  Preparation  and  AI  Literacy  
The  UNC  System  supports  AI  literacy  through  Faculty  Learning  Communities  and  instructional  guidance  
(UNC
 
System
 
Office,
 
Faculty
 
Learning
 
Communities
).
 
ECSU  aligns  with  this  priority  through  participation  in  AI-related  educational  initiatives  and  instructional  
programs
 
integrating
 
emerging
 
technologies.
 
 
3.  Alignment  of  Core  Principles  
Across  UNC  guidance  and  ECSU  practice,  the  following  principles  consistently  appear:  
●  Human  responsibility  for  AI  outputs  ●  Transparency  and  disclosure  ●  Protection  of  confidential  and  student  data  ●  Faculty  authority  at  the  course  level  

[PAGE 5]
●  Workforce  preparation  and  AI  literacy  
These  shared  themes  demonstrate  that  ECSU’s  approach  aligns  with  UNC  System  expectations  even  
without
 
a
 
standalone
 
AI
 
policy.
 
 
Works  Cited  
Elizabeth  City  State  University.  Student  Code  of  Conduct.  https://www.ecsu.edu/student-life/student-conduct.php 
Elizabeth  City  State  University  Library.  Academic  Integrity  and  Citation  Guidance.  https://libguides.ecsu.edu 
University  of  North  Carolina  at  Chapel  Hill.  Developing  AI  Guidance  for  Faculty.  https://ai.unc.edu/ai-guidance-for-faculty/ 
University  of  North  Carolina  at  Chapel  Hill.  Teaching  Generative  AI  Usage  Guidance.  https://ai.unc.edu/teaching-generative-ai-usage-guidance-2/ 
University  of  North  Carolina  System  Office.  Faculty  Learning  Communities.  https://www.northcarolina.edu/offices-and-services/academic-affairs/learning-and-technology/faculty-learning-communities/
